lines.speaker: FIRST SERVINGMAN
lines.text: Where’s Potpan, that he helps not to take away? He shift a

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVINGMAN
lines.text: trencher? he scrape a trencher!

lines.speaker: SECOND SERVINGMAN
lines.text: When good manners shall lie all in one or two men’s hands

lines.speaker: SECOND SERVINGMAN
lines.text: and they unwashed too, ’tis a foul thing.

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVINGMAN
lines.text: Away with the joint-stools, remove the court-cupboard, look to the plate. Good

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVINGMAN
lines.text: thou, save me a piece of marchpane; and, as thou lovest me,

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVINGMAN
lines.text: let the porter let in Susan Grindstone and Nell. Antony, and Potpan!

lines.speaker: SECOND SERVINGMAN
lines.text: Ay, boy, ready.

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVINGMAN
lines.text: You are looked for and called for, asked for and sought for,

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVINGMAN
lines.text: in the great chamber.

lines.speaker: SECOND SERVINGMAN
lines.text: We cannot be here and there too. Cheerly, boys; be brisk awhile,

lines.speaker: SECOND SERVINGMAN
lines.text: and the longer liver take all.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Welcome, gentlemen! ladies that have their toes

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Unplagued with corns will have a bout with you.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Ah ha, my mistresses! which of you all

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Will now deny to dance? she that makes dainty,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: She, I’ll swear, hath corns; am I come near ye now?

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Welcome, gentlemen! I have seen the day

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: That I have worn a visor and could tell

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: A whispering tale in a fair lady’s ear,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Such as would please: ’tis gone, ’tis gone, ’tis gone:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: You are welcome, gentlemen! come, musicians, play.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: A hall, a hall! give room! and foot it, girls.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: More light, you knaves; and turn the tables up,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And quench the fire, the room is grown too hot.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Ah, sirrah, this unlook’d-for sport comes well.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Nay, sit, nay, sit, good cousin Capulet;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: For you and I are past our dancing days:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: How long is’t now since last yourself and I

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Were in a mask?

lines.speaker: SECOND CAPULET
lines.text: By’r lady, thirty years.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: What, man! ’tis not so much, ’tis not so much:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: ’Tis since the nuptials of Lucentio,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Come Pentecost as quickly as it will,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Some five and twenty years; and then we mask’d.

lines.speaker: SECOND CAPULET
lines.text: ’Tis more, ’tis more: his son is elder, sir;

lines.speaker: SECOND CAPULET
lines.text: His son is thirty.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Will you tell me that?

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: His son was but a ward two years ago.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To a Servingman. What lady is that, which doth enrich the hand

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Of yonder knight?

lines.speaker: SERVINGMAN
lines.text: I know not, sir.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O, she doth teach the torches to burn bright!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: It seems she hangs upon the cheek of night

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Like a rich jewel in an Ethiope’s ear;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Beauty too rich for use, for earth too dear!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: So shows a snowy dove trooping with crows,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: As yonder lady o’er her fellows shows.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: The measure done, I’ll watch her place of stand,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And, touching hers, make blessed my rude hand.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Did my heart love till now? forswear it, sight!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: For I ne’er saw true beauty till this night.

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: This, by his voice, should be a Montague.

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Fetch me my rapier, boy. What dares the slave,

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Come hither, cover’d with an antic face,

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: To fleer and scorn at our solemnity?

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Now, by the stock and honour of my kin,

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: To strike him dead I hold it not a sin.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Why, how now, kinsman! wherefore storm you so?

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Uncle, this is a Montague, our foe,

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: A villain that is hither come in spite,

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: To scorn at our solemnity this night.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Young Romeo is it?

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: ’Tis he, that villain Romeo.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Content thee, gentle coz, let him alone;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: He bears him like a portly gentleman;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And, to say truth, Verona brags of him

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: To be a virtuous and well govern’d youth:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: I would not for the wealth of all the town

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Here in my house do him disparagement:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Therefore be patient, take no note of him:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: It is my will, the which if thou respect,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Show a fair presence and put off these frowns,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: An ill-beseeming semblance for a feast.

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: It fits, when such a villain is a guest:

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: I’ll not endure him.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: He shall be endured:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: What, goodman boy! I say, he shall: go to;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Am I the master here, or you? go to.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: You’ll not endure him! God shall mend my soul!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: You’ll make a mutiny among my guests!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: You will set cock-a-hoop! you’ll be the man!

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Why, uncle, ’tis a shame.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Go to, go to;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: You are a saucy boy: is’t so, indeed?

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: This trick may chance to scathe you, I know what:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: You must contrary me! marry, ’tis time.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Well said, my hearts! You are a princox; go:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Be quiet, or—More light, more light! For shame!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: I’ll make you quiet. What, cheerly, my hearts!

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Patience perforce with wilful choler meeting

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Makes my flesh tremble in their different greeting.

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: I will withdraw: but this intrusion shall

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Now seeming sweet convert to bitter gall.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To Juliet. If I profane with my unworthiest hand

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: This holy shrine, the gentle fine is this:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: My lips, two blushing pilgrims, ready stand

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To smooth that rough touch with a tender kiss.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Good pilgrim, you do wrong your hand too much,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Which mannerly devotion shows in this;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: For saints have hands that pilgrims’ hands do touch,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And palm to palm is holy palmers’ kiss.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Have not saints lips, and holy palmers too?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Ay, pilgrim, lips that they must use in prayer.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O, then, dear saint, let lips do what hands do;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: They pray, grant thou, lest faith turn to despair.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Saints do not move, though grant for prayers’ sake.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Then move not, while my prayer’s effect I take.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thus from my lips, by yours, my sin is purged.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Then have my lips the sin that they have took.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Sin from thy lips? O trespass sweetly urged!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Give me my sin again.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: You kiss by the book.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Madam, your mother craves a word with you.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What is her mother?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Marry, bachelor,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Her mother is the lady of the house,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: And a good lady, and a wise and virtuous:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I nursed her daughter, that you talk’d withal;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I tell you, he that can lay hold of her

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Shall have the chinks.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Is she a Capulet?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O dear account! my life is my foe’s debt.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Away, be gone; the sport is at the best.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Ay, so I fear; the more is my unrest.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Nay, gentlemen, prepare not to be gone;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: We have a trifling foolish banquet towards.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Is it e’en so? why, then, I thank you all;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: I thank you, honest gentlemen; good night.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: More torches here! Come on then, let’s to bed.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Ah, sirrah, by my fay, it waxes late:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: I’ll to my rest.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Come hither, nurse. What is yond gentleman?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: The son and heir of old Tiberio.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What’s he that now is going out of door?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Marry, that, I think, be young Petrucio.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What’s he that follows there, that would not dance?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I know not.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Go ask his name: if he be married,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: My grave is like to be my wedding bed.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: His name is Romeo, and a Montague;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: The only son of your great enemy.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: My only love sprung from my only hate!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Too early seen unknown, and known too late!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Prodigious birth of love it is to me,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That I must love a loathed enemy.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: What’s this? what’s this?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: A rhyme I learn’d even now

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Of one I danced withal. One calls within “Juliet.”

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Anon, anon!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Come, let’s away; the strangers all are gone.

stage_directions.text: Musicians waiting. Enter Servingmen with napkins.
Enter Capulet, with Juliet and others of his house, meeting the Guests and Maskers.
Exeunt all but Juliet and Nurse.

Juliet's hand, imagined as a saint's relic

annotations.title: The shared sonnet — the most formally intricate fourteen lines in the play
annotations.body: When Romeo and Juliet first speak to each other, they do not speak in ordinary dialogue: they construct, line by line, a perfect English sonnet, shared between two voices. Romeo opens with the first quatrain (89–92), Juliet takes the second (93–96), and the sestet concludes with their voices alternating. The rhymes interlock across speakers — hand/stand, much/touch, lips/tips, prayer/despair, sake/take. This is not a decorative gesture. It is a structural proof of mutual recognition: the sonnet form is the Renaissance's vocabulary for solitary male desire (Petrarch to Laura, unreciprocated); by making the sonnet shared, Shakespeare insists that this love is, from the start, mutual in a way Romeo's earlier Rosaline-complaint never was. The conceit of the poem — pilgrim at a saint's shrine, hands in prayer, kiss as absolution — casts Juliet as the sacred object but also makes her an equal partner in theological argument. She counters Romeo's every turn of image. The result is both the most beautiful love-exchange in English drama and a quiet philosophical claim: love is a shared language, not a monologue.
sources: Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)

annotations.title: Pilgrim and saint — love as religious parody
annotations.body: The shared sonnet runs on an extended metaphor: Romeo as pilgrim, Juliet as saint, his lips as 'two blushing pilgrims,' her hand as a 'holy shrine,' their kiss as absolution from sin. This is Petrarchan devotion ('my lady' as 'my saint') intensified to near-blasphemy. The language is borrowed from Catholic pilgrimage vocabulary — palmers (pilgrims who carried palm leaves from Jerusalem), shrines, prayer — which in post-Reformation England was both exotic and faintly illicit. When Juliet says wryly 'You kiss by th' book' (1.5.107), she is both charmed and critical: Romeo is kissing according to a courtly-love manual. The exchange is the play's most honest moment about its own poetic strategy — love performed by the rulebook, but the rulebook made alive by the partner who turns each move back on its author.

annotations.title: 'Prodigious birth of love' — Juliet's last line of the scene
annotations.body: When Juliet discovers from the Nurse that Romeo is a Montague, she gives the scene's closing quatrain: 'My only love sprung from my only hate! / Too early seen unknown, and known too late! / Prodigious birth of love it is to me, / That I must love a loathed enemy.' These rhyming couplets mirror the prologue's closing couplets — love and doom already inscribed. 'Prodigious' means 'portending evil' as well as 'wonderful': the word carries both senses. Notice that Juliet, rather than Romeo, names the structural danger. The whole first meeting has been built on her interrupting, countering, and finishing Romeo's poetic figures; here too, she gives the tragedy its first name. Juliet is the play's keenest judge of its own situation.

trials.prompt: What verse form do Romeo and Juliet construct together during their first conversation?
options: A couplet
A shared English sonnet — fourteen lines, three quatrains and a couplet, split between the two voices
Blank verse
Prose

trials.prompt: What controlling metaphor runs through the shared sonnet?
options: Hunter and prey
Pilgrim approaching a saint at a shrine — with kisses figured as absolution
Ship and storm
Gardener and rose

trials.prompt: Why does the shared sonnet matter as a structural claim about this love vs. the Rosaline-love of 1.1?
options: It shows Romeo has a better tailor
The sonnet has two voices instead of one — mutuality instead of Petrarchan unrequited monologue
It proves Romeo has memorized more poems
It has nothing to do with Rosaline

trials.prompt: What does Juliet mean by calling Romeo's style 'You kiss by th' book'?
options: He is very bad at kissing
He is kissing according to a courtly-love manual — charming and affected at once
He has a Bible in his hand
She is rejecting him

trials.prompt: Juliet closes the scene with 'Prodigious birth of love it is to me, / That I must love a loathed enemy.' What does this accomplish?
options: It breaks the scene's mood
It echoes the Prologue's closing couplets and names the tragic structure — Juliet is the first to articulate it
It is spoken only to herself and has no bearing on plot
It praises the Nurse
